action.content:
You are a field linguist. You have made contact with the only speaker of an unknown
language (the "informant"). Nobody has ever written down this language before. Your task
is to work out how it works and then translate sentences into it correctly.

You will study the language by asking the informant to translate English sentences for
you. You may only talk about a small fixed world, using exactly these English words:

Nouns: bird, spear, tree, farmer, hunter, lake, dog, hill, stone, boat, king, ship, road, river
Adjectives: blue, cold, old, tall, dark, big, small, red
Verbs (write them in this base form): watch, find, see, hear, carry, feed, chase, hold
Articles: the (definite), a (indefinite)
Number words: two, three, four, five, six (no number word = one / singular)
These are ALL the words in play - the language has no other words.

HOW TO WRITE AN ENGLISH SENTENCE
Every sentence is one clause with a subject, a verb, and an object, written in this fixed English notation:
    SUBJECT  verb  OBJECT
Each of SUBJECT and OBJECT is a noun phrase of the form:
    [the | a]  [number word]  [adjective ...]  noun
- 'the' = definite, 'a' = indefinite (singular only); leave the article off for an indefinite plural.
- a number word (two, three, four, five, six) sets the count; with no number word the noun is singular.
- zero or more adjectives may come before the noun.
Examples of well-formed English sentences you may send:
    a bird watch a spear
    six birds watch a spear
    a blue bird watch a spear

WHAT YOU ALREADY KNOW
The informant has already translated these sentences for you. Each line is
"<English>  =>  <the language>":
    a king find the four dark lakes  =>  bukiku goho hipopepila bapi bese
    the three big farmers carry two big hunters  =>  gugopila sapaba ha pelo habupi sapaba ki
    the four big spears carry the six hunters  =>  lulapila sapaba bese pelo habupila bihe
    the two small boats hold the four dogs  =>  besupila pugu ki hepo kupesupila bese
    three cold farmers see a dog  =>  gugopi hukobu ha pegu kupesu
    five hunters find the three hills  =>  habupi bi goho kabopila ha
    the two blue rivers hold the six kings  =>  lipipila biko ki hepo bukikupila bihe
    four cold lakes hear the six rivers  =>  hipopepi hukobu bese pusosa lipipila bihe
    four blue birds find the two cold spears  =>  gisipi biko bese goho lulapila hukobu ki
    the three tall hunters chase five tall lakes  =>  habupila lahabo ha bogi hipopepi lahabo bi
    four ships feed a small tree  =>  kepapi bese puke biho pugu
    the four boats hold a red road  =>  besupila bese hepo kala sihe
    the four red birds chase a stone  =>  gisipila sihe bese bogi hakula
    six old farmers chase the dark hunter  =>  gugopi kohi bihe bogi habula bapi
    the five lakes watch the five big farmers  =>  hipopepila bi hahibi gugopila sapaba bi

HOW THE SESSION WORKS
1. INVESTIGATION: You have 5 rounds of questioning. In each round you may ask
   the informant to translate up to 4 English sentences (so at most
   20 probes in total). The informant answers each one truthfully and
   exactly. Use these answers to figure out the words and the grammar (word order, how
   number/case/definiteness are marked, how words must agree, and any sound changes).
   Choose your probes deliberately - test specific hypotheses.
2. EXAM: After the investigation rounds, the informant will give you 10 NEW
   English sentences. You must translate each one INTO the language. Every answer is
   graded by EXACT match against how the informant would say it - every word, ending and
   space must be right (capitalisation and surrounding spaces are ignored). The exam
   sentences will use the same vocabulary but in combinations you have not been shown, so
   you must induce the general rules, not just memorise examples.

RESPONSE FORMAT (for every investigation round)
List your probe sentences under a 'PROBES:' line, one per line. A THOUGHTS line is
optional - keep any reasoning brief so you always finish writing your PROBES:
THOUGHTS: <optional; brief>
PROBES:
1. <an English sentence, written as described above>
2. <another English sentence>
(up to 4 lines; at least one)

Begin your first round of probes now.
action.label: context

action.content:
THOUGHTS: I need to systematically investigate this unknown language. From the provided examples, I can see patterns emerging but need to test specific hypotheses about: (1) word order (SVO vs SOV vs VSO), (2) how definiteness is marked (the vs a), (3) how plurality is marked, (4) how adjectives attach to nouns, (5) verb forms and any agreement, and (6) sound changes when words combine. Let me start with basic tests of word order and morphological patterns.

PROBES:
1. a bird find a stone
2. the bird find a stone
3. a bird find the stone
4. a stone find a bird
action.label: response

action.content:
TRANSLATIONS:
1. "a bird find a stone"  =>  gisi goho hakula
2. "the bird find a stone"  =>  gisila goho hakula
3. "a bird find the stone"  =>  gisi goho hakulala
4. "a stone find a bird"  =>  hakula goho gisi
STATUS: investigation round 1 of 5 done. Probes used: 4 of 20. 4 investigation round(s) left before the exam (10 sentences).
Send your next probes now (a PROBES list; brief THOUGHTS optional).
action.label: context

action.content:
THOUGHTS: Excellent! I can see clear patterns: (1) Word order is SVO (subject-verb-object). (2) "-la" marks definiteness on nouns (the bird = gisila, the stone = hakulala). (3) "-la" also appears on verbs in some contexts. (4) The verb "find" is "goho". Now I need to test: number marking, adjective placement, verb forms for different verbs, and whether definiteness marks on the verb.

PROBES:
1. a bird watch a stone
2. a bird see a stone
3. a bird hear a stone
4. two birds find a stone
action.label: response

action.content:
TRANSLATIONS:
1. "a bird watch a stone"  =>  gisi hahibi hakula
2. "a bird see a stone"  =>  gisi pegu hakula
3. "a bird hear a stone"  =>  gisi pusosa hakula
4. "two birds find a stone"  =>  gisipi ki goho hakula
STATUS: investigation round 2 of 5 done. Probes used: 8 of 20. 3 investigation round(s) left before the exam (10 sentences).
Send your next probes now (a PROBES list; brief THOUGHTS optional).
action.label: context

action.content:
THOUGHTS: Great progress! Now I have: watch=hahibi, see=pegu, hear=pusosa. For number: "two birds" = "gisipi ki" - so "-pi" marks plural and "ki" seems to be the number marker for two. I need to test: (1) other numbers (three, four, five, six), (2) adjective placement and morphology, (3) whether adjectives also get plural/definite markers, (4) verb definiteness marking, and (5) the remaining verbs.

PROBES:
1. three birds find a stone
2. four birds find a stone
3. five birds find a stone
4. six birds find a stone
action.label: response

action.content:
TRANSLATIONS:
1. "three birds find a stone"  =>  gisipi ha goho hakula
2. "four birds find a stone"  =>  gisipi bese goho hakula
3. "five birds find a stone"  =>  gisipi bi goho hakula
4. "six birds find a stone"  =>  gisipi bihe goho hakula
STATUS: investigation round 3 of 5 done. Probes used: 12 of 20. 2 investigation round(s) left before the exam (10 sentences).
Send your next probes now (a PROBES list; brief THOUGHTS optional).
action.label: context

action.content:
THOUGHTS: Now I have the number words: ha=three, bese=four, bi=five, bihe=six, ki=two. And "-pi" marks plural. So the pattern is: noun-stem + -pi (plural) + number-word. Now I need to test: (1) adjective placement and marking, (2) whether adjectives get plural/definite markers, (3) verb definiteness marking on the verb, and (4) remaining verbs (carry, feed, chase, hold).

PROBES:
1. a blue bird find a stone
2. the blue bird find a stone
3. two blue birds find a stone
4. the bird carry a stone
action.label: response

probes: a blue bird find a stone
the blue bird find a stone
two blue birds find a stone

action.content:
TRANSLATIONS:
1. "a blue bird find a stone"  =>  gisi biko goho hakula
2. "the blue bird find a stone"  =>  gisila biko goho hakula
3. "two blue birds find a stone"  =>  gisipi biko ki goho hakula
4. "the bird carry a stone"  =>  gisila pelo hakula
STATUS: investigation round 4 of 5 done. Probes used: 16 of 20. 1 investigation round(s) left before the exam (10 sentences).
Send your next probes now (a PROBES list; brief THOUGHTS optional).
action.label: context

action.content:
THOUGHTS: Great! I see: (1) Adjectives come BEFORE the noun (gisi biko = blue bird). (2) Adjectives don't get plural/definite markers - they stay the same. (3) Definiteness "-la" goes on the noun, not the adjective. (4) "carry" = "pelo". Now I need to test: (1) remaining verbs (feed, chase, hold), (2) whether definiteness marks on the object noun, (3) adjective morphology with other adjectives, and (4) any verb agreement or definiteness marking on the verb itself.

PROBES:
1. a bird feed a stone
2. a bird chase a stone
3. a bird hold a stone
4. the blue bird find the stone
action.label: response

probes: the blue bird find the stone

action.content:
TRANSLATIONS:
1. "a bird feed a stone"  =>  gisi puke hakula
2. "a bird chase a stone"  =>  gisi bogi hakula
3. "a bird hold a stone"  =>  gisi hepo hakula
4. "the blue bird find the stone"  =>  gisila biko goho hakulala

FINAL EXAM. Investigation is over. Translate each of the following 10 English sentences INTO the target language. Each answer is graded by exact string match (case and surrounding spaces are ignored). Every sentence uses only words you have already seen translated.

1. the six hills chase three cold hills
2. a bird hold three rivers
3. four tall trees hear the five dark spears
4. the four big kings feed five big farmers
5. six ships watch the hill
6. the four kings watch four tall hunters
7. four hills watch the dark dog
8. a road chase the two blue spears
9. a blue farmer chase the three ships
10. the tall ship watch the two dark trees

Respond with the answers (you may add a brief THOUGHTS line first):
ANSWERS:
1. <translation of sentence 1>
... through 10.
action.label: context

action.content:
THOUGHTS: Now I have all the verb translations: find=goho, watch=hahibi, see=pegu, hear=pusosa, carry=pelo, feed=puke, chase=bogi, hold=hepo. Definiteness "-la" goes on the noun. Plural "-pi" goes on the noun. Number words: ki=two, ha=three, bese=four, bi=five, bihe=six. Adjectives come before the noun and don't get number/definiteness markers. Word order is SVO. Let me translate each sentence:

1. "the six hills chase three cold hills" - hills=ha, cold=hukobu, chase=bogi. So: halapila bihe bogi ha hukobu ha
2. "a bird hold three rivers" - bird=gisi, hold=hepo, three=ha, river=lipi. So: gisi hepo ha lipipi ha
3. "four tall trees hear the five dark spears" - tree=biho, tall=lahabo, four=bese, hear=pusosa, five=bi, dark=bapi, spear=lula. So: bihopi lahabo bese pusosa lulapila bapi bi
4. "the four big kings feed five big farmers" - king=bukiku, four=bese, big=sapa, feed=puke, five=bi, farmer=gugu. So: bukikupila sapa bese puke gugupi sapa bi
5. "six ships watch the hill" - ship=kepi, six=bihe, watch=hahibi, hill=ha. So: kepipi bihe hahibi halala
6. "the four kings watch four tall hunters" - king=bukiku, four=bese, watch=hahibi, hunter=habi, tall=lahabo. So: bukikupila bese hahibi habipi lahabo bese
7. "four hills watch the dark dog" - hill=ha, four=bese, watch=hahibi, dark=bapi, dog=kupu. So: halapi bese hahibi kupula bapi
8. "a road chase the two blue spears" - road=sihe, chase=bogi, two=ki, blue=biko, spear=lula. So: sihe bogi lulapila biko ki
9. "a blue farmer chase the three ships" - farmer=gugu, blue=biko, chase=bogi, three=ha, ship=kepi. So: gugu biko bogi kepipila ha
10. "the tall ship watch the two dark trees" - ship=kepi, tall=lahabo, watch=hahibi, two=ki, dark=bapi, tree=biho. So: kepila lahabo hahibi bihopila bapi ki

ANSWERS:
1. halapila bihe bogi ha hukobu ha
2. gisi hepo ha lipipi ha
3. bihopi lahabo bese pusosa lulapila bapi bi
4. bukikupila sapa bese puke gugupi sapa bi
5. kepipi bihe hahibi halala
6. bukikupila bese hahibi habipi lahabo bese
7. halapi bese hahibi kupula bapi
8. sihe bogi lulapila biko ki
9. gugu biko bogi kepipila ha
10. kepila lahabo hahibi bihopila bapi ki
action.label: response

answers: halapila bihe bogi ha hukobu ha
bihopi lahabo bese pusosa lulapila bapi bi
bukikupila sapa bese puke gugupi sapa bi
bukikupila bese hahibi habipi lahabo bese
kepila lahabo hahibi bihopila bapi ki
targets: kabopila bihe bogi kabopi hukobu ha
bihopi lahabo bese pusosa lulapila bapi bi
bukikupila sapaba bese puke gugopi sapaba bi
bukikupila bese hahibi habupi lahabo bese
kepala lahabo hahibi bihopila bapi ki
